one_line_takeaway: Invisible white-text prompt injections hidden in submitted PDFs can push all three tested frontier-model AI paper reviewers toward near-maximum scores (DeepSeek and Gemini reaching ~10/10 within 3 iterative optimization rounds), and while a simple LLM-based detect-and-warn defense catches 99% of naive attacks, an attacker who explicitly optimizes the injection to evade that same detector cuts detection to 24% and still lifts average scores from 7.06 to 8.11.
key_finding: All three reviewer models are vulnerable to varying degrees. Static attacks (Table 2): relative to no-attack baselines (Gemini 7.03, DeepSeek 7.06, GPT-5 5.73, vs. human average 5.12), the strongest static prompt (Prompt 3, combined) raised average scores by +1.91 (Gemini, to 8.94) and +2.80 (DeepSeek, to 9.86), but only +0.20 (GPT-5, to 5.93); GPT-5's largest static-attack gain instead came from Prompt 4 (detailed outline), reaching 6.97 (the paper's prose calls this a +1.60 gain, though Table 2's own values give +1.24); DeepSeek-Chat was consistently the most vulnerable, GPT-5 the most resistant. Iterative attacks were substantially stronger: within 3 rounds they drove Gemini to 9.75-9.84 and DeepSeek to 9.94-10.00 (i.e., essentially maximum score), while GPT-5 rose only to 6.76-6.99 (Table 2, Table 4). Attack effectiveness was robust to injection position (Table 3: top-of-first-page 8.97, bottom-of-middle-page 8.90, bottom-of-last-page 8.94, all far above the 7.03 baseline on Gemini) and held across the full range of human ratings and paper lengths (Figures 3-4). Cross-model transferability (Table 5) showed prompts optimized against GPT-5 transferred best to other reviewers (+2.19 on Gemini, +2.72 on DeepSeek), prompts from DeepSeek-Chat transferred worst (+0.15 gain on GPT-5), and GPT-5 was the most robust target overall (+1.04 when attacked with a Gemini-optimized prompt, +0.15 with a DeepSeek-optimized prompt). Absent attacks, AI-human score correlation was moderate: GPT-5 r=0.643 (p<0.001, avg 5.37, closest to human avg 5.12), DeepSeek r=0.501 (p<0.001, avg 7.03), Gemini r=0.477/0.478 (p<0.001, avg 7.05) -- both DeepSeek and Gemini rated systematically higher than humans even without attacks. The detection-based defense (Table 6, tested on DeepSeek-Chat) detected 99/100 attack instances and fully recovered the injected text in 91/100, cutting the average attacked score from 10.00 back to 7.27 (vs. 7.06 no-attack baseline, difference not significant, t-test p=0.26); however 25/100 cases still scored more than 1.5 points above baseline despite detection (partial defense failure) and 17/100 scored more than 1.5 points below baseline (apparent overcorrection). The adaptive attack explicitly designed to evade the defense (Table 7) was substantially more effective against the defended reviewer: it raised the average score to 8.11 (vs 7.06 baseline) while the detector's detection rate collapsed to 24/100 (recovery 20/100); 30/100 papers scored more than 1.5 points above baseline, of which 25 were undetected (including 9 reaching perfect scores) and only 5 were detected-but-under-mitigated, versus just 1 case of overcorrection below baseline.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it calls the first systematic, large-scale, quantitative study of 'In-Paper Prompt Injection' (IPI) attacks on AI-based scientific paper reviewers. It introduces two attack paradigms the authors designed and ran themselves: (1) a static attack that embeds one of four fixed malicious prompts (positive-review, accept-paper, combined, detailed-outline) as invisible white-colored microscopic text into a submitted PDF, and (2) an iterative attack that optimizes the injection prompt over up to three rounds against a simulated reviewer model, using the previous round's injected prompt and the reviewer's returned review/score as feedback to a prompt-generation attacker LLM, with early stopping if a maximum score (10) is reached. The authors evaluate both attacks against three commercial frontier-model AI reviewers (GPT-5, DeepSeek-Chat/DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-2.5-Pro, accessed via OpenRouter) on 100 randomly sampled ICLR 2025 submissions with real human peer reviews as ground truth. They also propose and test a detection-based defense (prompting the reviewer to first flag and quote suspected injected instructions before scoring) and an adaptive attack designed explicitly to evade that defense.
threat_model: Attacker: a paper author (or anyone controlling a submitted PDF) who wants to manipulate an AI-assisted peer-review pipeline into producing an inflated score/recommendation. Attack vector: adversarial text embedded directly in the PDF file, rendered in white color and microscopic font so it is invisible to human readers on the rendered page but is extracted and processed by the PDF-to-text pipeline (OpenRouter's pdf-text engine in this paper) feeding the AI reviewer LLM. No access to model weights or APIs beyond normal black-box prompting/querying is assumed; the iterative attack requires only query access to a simulated/surrogate reviewer to obtain a score signal for optimization. Defender: the AI review system itself, which can be augmented with a detection-based prompt that instructs the reviewer LLM to identify and quote suspected injected instructions before assigning a score. The paper explicitly does not address detection at the PDF-parsing/rendering layer (e.g., stripping invisible text before it reaches the LLM) -- its defense operates purely at the LLM-prompt level.
limitations: The authors explicitly state, in a dedicated Limitations section, that due to the high API cost of generating AI-based reviews (tens of thousands of tokens per paper), the study is restricted to 100 papers and 3 reviewer models, leaving evaluation on a larger set of papers and reviewers to future work. Additional limitations evident from the methodology (not all flagged by the authors as such): the defense is a single simple detect-then-warn prompting strategy, not compared against alternative defenses (e.g., PDF-layer sanitization of invisible/hidden text, retrieval-based filtering, or ensembling); the iterative and adaptive attacks were only tested against a fairly narrow harness (OpenRouter pdf-text extraction plus a specific rubric prompt), so results may not generalize to production review platforms with different PDF parsing or reviewer prompting; and the adaptive attack was evaluated only against DeepSeek-Chat with the same detection defense used in Table 6, not repeated for GPT-5 or Gemini.

datasets_used: 100 randomly sampled ICLR 2025 submissions (PDFs + full peer reviews) drawn from 20,000+ OpenReview ICLR 2025 submissions
quantitative_results: Static Attack Prompt 3 (combined) raised average overall score: Gemini-2.5-Pro 7.03->8.94 (+1.91), DeepSeek-Chat 7.06->9.86 (+2.80), GPT-5 5.73->5.93 (+0.20); GPT-5's largest static-attack gain was from Prompt 4 (detailed outline), 5.73->6.97 (+1.24 by table arithmetic; the paper's prose states +1.60 for this same comparison, a discrepancy between Table 2 and the text)
Iterative Attack (Prompt 3-based, 3 rounds): Gemini-2.5-Pro 8.94->9.84, DeepSeek-Chat 9.86->10.00, GPT-5 5.93->6.99
Injection position robustness on Gemini-2.5-Pro under Prompt 3: top-of-first-page 8.97, bottom-of-middle-page 8.90, bottom-of-last-page 8.94 (baseline 7.03)

Cross-model transfer of iterative attack prompts: GPT-5-generated prompts gave +2.19 on Gemini-2.5-Pro and +2.72 on DeepSeek-Chat; DeepSeek-Chat-generated prompts gave only +0.15 on GPT-5; Gemini-generated prompts gave +1.04 on GPT-5
Human vs AI score correlation (no attack): GPT-5 r=0.643 (p<0.001, avg 5.37), DeepSeek-Chat r=0.501 (p<0.001, avg 7.03), Gemini-2.5-Pro r=0.477 (p<0.001, avg 7.05); human average 5.12

Detection-based defense (DeepSeek-Chat, Prompt-3-based iterative attack): no-attack baseline 7.06, attacked-no-defense 10.00, attacked-with-defense 7.27 (t-test vs baseline p=0.26); attack detection rate 99/100 (99%); full prompt recovery 91/100 (91%); 25/100 cases still scored >1.5 above baseline, 17/100 scored >1.5 below baseline

Adaptive attack vs defended DeepSeek-Chat reviewer: baseline 7.06 -> adaptive-attack average 8.11; detection rate collapsed to 24/100 (24%), full recovery 20/100 (20%); 30/100 papers scored >1.5 above baseline (25 undetected, incl. 9 perfect scores; 5 detected-but-under-mitigated); only 1 case >1.5 below baseline
baselines_compared: Original paper / no-attack score (per reviewer model)
Human peer-review average score (5.12) as an external reference baseline
Static Attack Prompts 1-4 (positive review, accept paper, combined, detailed outline) compared against no-attack and against each other
Iterative attack (Prompt-1-seeded and Prompt-3-seeded) compared against static attacks and no-attack
Attacked-with-defense score compared against attacked-no-defense and no-attack scores
Adaptive (defense-evading) attack compared against no-attack baseline and against the non-adaptive attack-with-defense condition
